channel.title: Mental Health on MinWeb
channel.description: Recent content in Mental Health on MinWeb

item.title: How Changing Your Self-Talk Can Transform Your Life
item.description:
Ever replay a conversation from three years ago or spiral into a “what if” scenario at 3:00 AM? You aren’t alone. Your brain is a newsroom that never closes, processing around 60,000 to 80,000 thoughts every single day.

Here is the kicker: up to 80% of those thoughts are negative. Even worse, 95% of them are just the same old loops on repeat. We aren’t generating fresh, innovative ideas; we’re stuck listening to a mental “greatest hits” album of our insecurities, fears, and past mistakes. It’s honestly exhausting.

item.title: Why "So What?" Is Not the Cure for Workplace Stress
item.description:
We’ve all been there. It’s 3:00 PM on a Tuesday. Your inbox is overflowing, your manager just added two more deliverables to your plate, and you can feel your heart rate climbing. You tell yourself to “just breathe,” but the anxiety doesn’t dissipate. It stays, humming in the background like a faulty fluorescent light.

A popular life hack suggests a simple mantra for these moments: just ask yourself, “So what difference does it make?”

item.title: The Case for Body Silence: Why You Should Stop Commenting
item.description:
Stop talking about people’s bodies. Even if you think you are being kind, you are crossing a line that has serious consequences for the other person’s health and mental well-being. Practicing Body Silence is the most respectful thing you can do for the people around you.

_IMG_PLACEHOLDER

Minding your own business is a public health intervention. When you comment on a body, you have no idea what that person is enduring. You might be triggering an eating disorder, insulting a grieving parent, or stressing someone out so much that their body enters a defensive, high-stress state.

item.title: Why You Should Stop Commenting on People’s Bodies
item.description:
The most important thing you can do for the people in your life is remarkably simple: mind your own business.

Stop commenting on bodies. This applies to the ones you think you are complimenting and the ones you find confusing. Even if you think you are being supportive, your words carry weight you cannot see and history you do not know.

_IMG_PLACEHOLDER

The unintended cost of your words

Saying “You look great, have you lost weight?” is not a harmless way to make someone feel good. A 2024 University of Bristol study found that weight teasing at age 13 predicts higher internalized weight stigma and poorer mental health twenty years later.
